blocks:
Ayahs

لَمْ يَكُنِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ مُنفَكِّينَ حَتَّىٰ تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَةُ (١)
The faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists were not set apart [from the community of the faithful] until the proof had come to them: (1)
رَسُولٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ يَتْلُوا۟ صُحُفًا مُّطَهَّرَةً (٢)
an apostle from Allah reciting impeccable scriptures, (2)
فِيهَا كُتُبٌ قَيِّمَةٌ (٣)
wherein are upright writings. (3)
وَمَا تَفَرَّقَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَةُ (٤)
Those who were given the Book did not divide except after the proof had come to them. (4)
وَمَآ أُمِرُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ حُنَفَآءَ وَيُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ دِينُ ٱلْقَيِّمَةِ (٥)
Though they were not commanded except to worship Allah, dedicating their faith to Him as men of pure faith, and to maintain the prayer and pay the zakat. That is the upright religion. (5)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ فِى نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ شَرُّ ٱلْبَرِيَّةِ (٦)
Indeed the faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists will be in the fire of hell, to remain in it [forever]. It is they who are the worst of creatures. (6)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ ٱلْبَرِيَّةِ (٧)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—it is they who are the best of creatures. (7)
جَزَآؤُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِىَ رَبَّهُۥ (٨)
Their reward, near their Lord, is the Gardens of Eden, with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. Allah is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him. That is for those who fear their Lord. (8)

(Commentary)

The Surah records the message of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to the generality of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) and the polytheists (المشركين)—in other words, to the followers of religions and others, who constitute the entirety of mankind. Thus, it conveys the universality of the message and that it was necessitated by the Divine Sunnah (السنة الإلهية)—the Sunnah of Guidance (سنة الهداية)—which is alluded to by such verses as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا هَدَيْنَاهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شَاكِرًا وَإِمَّا كَفُورًا "Indeed We have guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful" (76:3), and His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلَا فِيهَا نَذِيرٌ "and there is not a nation but a warner has passed in it" (35:24). It argues for the universality of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) mission by demonstrating that it contains nothing but that which rectifies human society in terms of belief and action, as will become clear, Allah willing.

The Surah could potentially be Meccan or Medinan, though its context (سياق) more closely resembles the Medinan revelations.

His saying, the Exalted: لَمْ يَكُنِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ مُنْفَكِّينَ حَتَّى تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ "The faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists were not going to leave [their ways] until there should come to them a clear proof" (98:1). The apparent meaning of the verses—which are in a context (سياق) indicating the establishment of the proof (قيام الحجة) against those who disbelieved in the Islamic mission among the People of the Book and the polytheists, and against those who were given the Book when discord arose among them—is an allusion to the fact that the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is one of the instances of the clear proof (الحجة البينة) established over people. The establishment of this proof is necessitated by the Divine Sunnah (السنة الإلهية) that governs His servants; it necessitated the coming of the clear proof (البينة) to them, just as it necessitated it before they became divided in their religion.

Accordingly, the "faithless" (الذين كفروا) in the verse refers to those who disbelieved in the prophetic Islamic mission from among the People of the Book and the polytheists. The word "from" (مِنْ) in His saying "from among the People of the Book" (مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ) is for partitivity (التبعيض), not for clarification (التبيين). His saying "and the polytheists" (وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ) is coordinated (عطف) with "the People of the Book," referring to those other than the People of the Book, such as idolaters and others.

His saying "going to leave" (مُنْفَكِّينَ) is derived from "disengagement" (الانفكاك), which is the separation from a state of intense connection. What is meant by it—as inferred from His saying "until there should come to them a clear proof"—is their disengagement from what the Sunnah of Guidance and Clarification (سنة الهداية والبيان) necessitates. It is as if the Divine Sunnah had taken hold of them and would not leave them until the clear proof (البينة) came to them; once the clear proof came, it left them to their own devices, as the Exalted said: وَمَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيُضِلَّ قَوْمًا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَاهُمْ حَتَّى يُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ مَا يَتَّقُونَ "Allah would not lead a people astray after He has guided them until He makes clear to them what they should beware of" (9:115).

His saying "until there should come to them a clear proof" (حَتَّى تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ) maintains its literal sense of futurity. The clear proof (البينة) is the manifest evidence (الحجة الظاهرة). The meaning is: those who disbelieved in the message of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), or his mission, or the Quran, were not to be disengaged until the clear proof came to them, and that clear proof is Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The commentators have an extraordinary disagreement regarding the interpretation of this verse and the meanings of its individual words, to the extent that some have said—as reported—that this verse is among the most difficult of Quranic verses in terms of composition and interpretation. However, the meaning we have provided is the one suited to its context (سياق) without contradiction between the verses or conflict between the sentences and words. Whoever wishes to see the details of what has been and is being said should consult the more extensive commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: رَسُولٌ مِنَ اللهِ يَتْلُوا صُحُفاً مُطَهَّرَةً فِيها كُتُبٌ قَيِّمَةٌ "an apostle from Allah reciting purified scriptures, containing upright writings" (98:2-3). This is an explanation of the "clear proof" (البينة), and it certainly refers to Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as indicated by the context.

The "scriptures" (الصحف) is the plural of sahifah (صحيفة), which is that upon which one writes. Here it refers to the revealed portions of the Quran. The term "scriptures" (الصحف) is frequently used in the speech of the Exalted to refer to parts of the Heavenly Books, including the Noble Quran. The Exalted said: فِي صُحُفٍ مُكَرَّمَةٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ مُطَهَّرَةٍ بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ كِرَامٍ بَرَرَةٍ "[It is recorded] in honored scriptures, exalted and purified, by the hands of scribes, noble and pious" (80:13-16).

The meaning of the scriptures being "purified" (مطهرة) is their being sanctified from the filth of falsehood (الباطل) through the touch of devils. The Exalted has repeatedly stated that it is a truth protected from the interference of devils, saying: لَا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ "no one touches it except the pure" (56:79).

His saying "containing upright writings" (فِيها كُتُبٌ قَيِّمَةٌ): "writings" (الكتب) is the plural of kitab (كتاب), meaning that which is written. It is applied to the tablet (اللوح), the parchment (القرطاس), and the like upon which words are inscribed; it is also applied to the words themselves which the inscriptions represent. Sometimes it is applied to the meanings (المعاني) insofar as they are represented by words. It is also applied to a ruling (الحكم) or a decree (القضاء); it is said "such was written (كُتب) upon him," meaning it was decreed that he do such. The Exalted said: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيَامُ "Prescribed for you is fasting" (2:183) and said: كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْقِتَالُ "Prescribed for you is fighting" (2:216).

It appears that the "writings" (الكتب) within the scriptures refer to the Divine rulings and decrees related to belief (الاعتقاد) and action (العمل). Evidence for this is their description as "upright" (قيمة), which is derived from "standing over something" (القيام بالشيء) in the sense of preserving it, observing its interests, and guaranteeing its felicity. The Exalted said: أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ ذَلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ "He has commanded that you worship none but Him. That is the upright religion" (12:40). It is well known that the Heavenly Scriptures only "stand over" (تقوم) the affairs of human society and preserve its interests through the rulings and decrees they contain regarding belief and action.

Thus, the meaning of the two verses is: The clear proof (الحجة البينة) that came to them is a Messenger from Allah who recites Heavenly scriptures purified from the taints of falsehood; in those scriptures are rulings and decrees that stand over the affairs of human society and preserve its interests.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما تَفَرَّقَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ "And those who were given the Book did not divide except after the clear proof had come to them" (98:4). The first verse, "The faithless from among the People of the Book..." etc., alluded to their disbelief in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his Book which contains the true mission. This verse, however, alludes to their division that preceded the Islamic mission. This has been mentioned in several places in the Noble Quran, as the Exalted said: وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ "Those who were given the Book did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (3:19), among other verses.

The coming of the "clear proof" (البينة) to them refers to the prophetic clarification (البيان النبوي) that was made clear to them in their Book or explained to them by their prophets. The Exalted said: وَلَمَّا جَاءَ عِيسَى بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُمْ بِالْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هَذَا صِرَاطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ فَاخْتَلَفَ الْأَحْزَابُ مِنْ بَيْنِهِمْ "When Jesus brought the clear proofs, he said, ‘I have certainly brought you wisdom, and [I have come] to make clear to you some of what you differ about. So be wary of Allah and obey me. Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path.’ But the factions differed among themselves" (43:63-65).

If you ask: Why did He address the division of the People of the Book and their splitting into sects, but did not address the division of the polytheists, their turning away from the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), and their denial of the message?

I say: It is not improbable that His saying "And those who were given the Book did not divide..." etc., includes the polytheists just as it includes the People of the Book. He substituted "those who were given the Book" (الذين أوتوا الكتاب) for "the People of the Book" (أهل الكتاب)—who in Quranic usage are the Jews, Christians, Sabaeans, and Magians, or just the Jews and Christians. The two expressions are distinct. The Exalted has explicitly stated that He sent down the Book—which is the prescribed law (الشريعة) governing their differences in life's affairs—at the first appearance of vital differences among them. Then they differed in religion after the truth had become clear to them and the proof (الحجة) had been established against them. Thus, Allah gave a Book to the generality of mankind, but they then differed regarding it; some forgot what they were given, some took it in a distorted form, and some preserved it and believed in it. The Exalted said: كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتَابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed. And none differed in it except those who had been given it, after the clear proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (2:213). The commentary on this verse has already passed.

In this same sense is His saying, the Exalted: تِلْكَ الرُّسُلُ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَى بَعْضٍ "These are the apostles, some of whom We gave an advantage over others..." until He said: وَلَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ مَا اقْتَتَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّنَاتُ وَلَكِنِ اخْتَلَفُوا فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ آمَنَ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ كَفَرَ "Had Allah wished, those who succeeded them would not have fought each other after the clear proofs had come to them. But they differed: some of them believed and some of them were faithless" (2:253).

In short, "those who were given the Book" (الذين أوتوا الكتاب) is more general than "the People of the Book" (أهل الكتاب). Therefore, His saying "And those who were given the Book did not divide..." etc., includes the polytheists just as it includes the People of the Book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ حُنَفاءَ "Yet they were not commanded except to worship Allah, dedicating their religion exclusively to Him, as hanifs" (98:5) etc. The pronoun in "they were commanded" (أُمِرُوا) refers to the faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists. That is, the message of the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the upright writings (الكتب القيمة) in the scriptures of revelation contained nothing except the command to worship Allah, the Exalted, with the condition of sincerity in religion (الإخلاص في الدين), such that they associate nothing with Him.

His saying "as hanifs" (حُنَفاءَ) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the plural pronoun. It is the plural of hanif (حنيف), derived from hanaf (الحنف), which is the inclination away from the extremes of excess (الإفراط) and deficiency (التفريط) toward the very center of moderation (الاعتدال). Allah, the Exalted, has named Islam a hanif religion because it commands moderation in all matters and the avoidance of excess and deficiency.

His saying "and to maintain the prayer and pay the zakat" (وَيُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَيُؤْتُوا الزَّكاةَ) is of the nature of mentioning the specific after the general, or the part after the whole, due to the importance of their status. Prayer (الصلاة) and zakat (الزكاة) are the pillars of Islam; they represent the specific devotional turning toward Allah and the spending of wealth for the sake of Allah.

His saying "and that is the upright religion" (وَذلِكَ دِينُ الْقَيِّمَةِ): that is, the religion of the upright writings (الكتب القيمة), as interpreted by some. If "upright writings" refers to all Heavenly Books—meaning the Book of Noah and the prophets after him (عليه السلام)—then the meaning is that what they were commanded and called to in the Muhammadan mission is the same religion they were charged with in their own upright books; it is not an innovation. The religion of Allah is one, and they must adhere to it because it is the upright one.

If it refers to the upright writings that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was reciting from the purified scriptures, then the meaning is that they were not commanded in the Islamic mission except with rulings and decrees that are upright (القيمة)—preserving the interests of human society. Thus, they have no choice but to believe in them and practice them.

In any case, the verse alludes to the fact that the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) contained in the Noble Quran—which confirms the Book before it and is a guardian (المهيمن) over it in what it commands human society—stands over their affairs and preserves the interests of their lives. This is explained most fully by His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ذَلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ "So set your face on the religion as a hanif, with the nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — in which He has created mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion" (30:30).

With this verse, the explanation of the universality of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) message and the inclusion of all mankind in the Islamic mission is completed. His saying "The faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists..." etc., alludes to the fact that it was necessary according to the Sunnah of Divine Guidance that the proof be established against whoever disbelieved in the mission from among the People of the Book and the polytheists. Although these were some of the People of the Book and the polytheists, it is necessary that there be no difference between some and others regarding the applicability of the mission; its applicability to some is inseparable from its applicability to all.

His saying "an apostle from Allah..." etc., alludes to the fact that this clear proof (البينة) is Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). His saying "And those... did not divide" etc., alludes to the fact that their division and their previous disbelief in the truth also occurred after the clear proof had come.

His saying "Yet they were not commanded except to worship Allah..." etc., conveys that what they were called to and commanded with is an upright religion (دين قيم) that preserves the interests of human society; therefore, they must all believe in it and not disbelieve.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتابِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ فِي نارِ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها أُولئِكَ هُمْ شَرُّ الْبَرِيَّةِ "Indeed the faithless from among the People of the Book and the polytheists will be in the fire of hell, to remain in it [forever]. It is they who are the worst of creatures" (98:6). Having finished alluding to their disbelief in the clear proof (البينة) necessitated by the Sunnah of Divine Guidance and what it called to of the upright religion, He begins the warning and the glad tidings—threatening the disbelievers and promising the believers. "Creatures" (البرية) means the creation (الخلق), and the meaning is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ أُولئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ "Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—it is they who are the best of creatures" (98:7). In this is a restriction (قصر) of "best-ness" to the righteous believers, just as the previous verse restricted "worst-ness" to the disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: جَزاؤُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ "Their reward, near their Lord..." until His saying: ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِيَ رَبَّهُ "That is for him who fears his Lord" (98:8). Eden (العدن) means stability and permanence; thus, the "Gardens of Eden" (جنات عدن) are gardens of eternity and duration. Describing them with His saying "to remain in them forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً) is an emphasis of what the name itself implies.

His saying "Allah is well-pleased with them" (رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنْهُمْ): Pleasure (الرضى) from the Exalted is an attribute of action (صفة فعل), and its concrete instance is the reward He has given them as a recompense for their faith and righteous deeds.

His saying "That is for him who fears his Lord" (ذلِكَ لِمَنْ خَشِيَ رَبَّهُ) is a set mark for the felicity of the Hereafter. The Exalted has said: إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْعُلَمَاءُ "Only those of Allah’s servants fear Him who possess knowledge" (35:28). Thus, knowledge of Allah entails fear (الخشية) of Him, and fear of Him entails faith in Him—in the sense of heart-felt commitment to His Lordship and Divinity—followed by righteous deeds.

Know that the commentators have intense disagreements and numerous opinions regarding the interpretation of the individual words of these verses, in which there is no benefit in addressing. Whoever wishes to examine them should consult the more extensive works.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in a narration from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "The clear proof (البينة) is Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Aisha that she said: "I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, who is the most noble of creation to Allah?' He said: 'O Aisha, do you not read: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ ـ أُولئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ (Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—it is they who are the best of creatures)?'"

In the same source, Ibn Asakir extracted from Jabir bin Abdullah who said: "We were with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when Ali approached. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'By Him in whose hand is my soul, indeed this man and his followers (شيعته) are the successful ones on the Day of Resurrection.' Then was revealed: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ ـ أُولئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ. Thus, the companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say when Ali approached: 'The best of creatures (خير البرية) has come.'"

I say: This meaning is also narrated from Ibn Adi from Ibn Abbas, and also from Ibn Mardawayh from Ali (peace be upon him). It is also narrated in al-Burhan from al-Muwaffaq bin Ahmad in the book al-Manaqib from Yazid bin Shurahil al-Ansari, the scribe of Ali, from him. Likewise in al-Majma', from the book Shawahid al-Tanzil by al-Hakim from Yazid bin Shurahil from him; its wording is: "I heard Ali saying: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) passed away while I was leaning him against my chest, and he said: "O Ali, have you not heard the saying of Allah: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ ـ أُولئِكَ هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ? They are your followers (شيعتك), and my meeting place with you is the Pond (الحوض)—when the nations gather for the reckoning, they will be called while their faces and limbs are radiant (غراً محجلين)."'"

In al-Majma', from Muqatil bin Sulayman from al-Dahhak from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: هُمْ خَيْرُ الْبَرِيَّةِ (they are the best of creatures), he said: "It was revealed concerning Ali and his household (أهل بيته)."
